title: Grounding the Final Answer
concept: Instructing the model to answer only from provided context rather than drawing on parametric memory.

exercises.prompt: Why is relying purely on a pre-trained LLM's general memory risky for business applications?

options.text: They can confidently state incorrect or fabricated facts.
They refuse to answer any questions at all.
They only speak in computer code.

exercises.explain: Welcome to Unit 459! When building real-world LLM apps, standard models often hallucinate facts. We need grounding to force them to stick to our trusted data sources.

cards.title: Stop the Guesswork
cards.body: Large Language Models are brilliant, but they love to make things up when they don't know the answer. By 'grounding' them, we force them to act like open-book test takers who can only write down what's on the page.

cards.title: What is Grounding?
cards.body: Grounding is the practice of restricting an LLM's answers to a specific, verified source of information (the context) rather than letting it use its own pre-trained memory. This dramatically reduces hallucinations and ensures answers are factually correct according to your documents.

cards.title: The Courtroom Witness
cards.body: Think of the LLM as a witness in a courtroom. Parametric memory is their personal gossip and general knowledge, while the provided context is the official folder of evidence. Grounding means ordering the witness to look ONLY at the evidence folder to answer, ignoring everything else they think they know.

cards.title: Grounded vs. Wild Answers
cards.body: If you ask an ungrounded model 'What is our refund policy?', it might guess '30 days' based on typical internet data. A grounded model, given a document stating 'All sales are final', will correctly answer 'All sales are final.'

cards.title: The Blend Trap
cards.body: Many believe that LLMs automatically prefer your provided context over their training data. In reality, without strict system instructions, models often blend the two, leading to subtle and dangerous errors.

cards.title: Information Sources
cards.body: Visualizing how information flows during a grounded query versus an ungrounded query.

left.text: Answers using vast, unpredictable internet training data.

right.text: Answers using only the specific document you pasted in.

exercises.prompt: What is the main goal of grounding a language model's final answer?

options.text: Fine-tuning a model on billions of new web pages.
Restricting the model to answer questions using only the provided context.
Connecting the model to an external database to run SQL queries automatically.
Formatting the final output as a clean JSON block.

exercises.explain: Grounding is the technique of anchoring a model's output directly to a verified source of truth (the context) provided in the prompt.

exercises.prompt: Is it a good grounding practice to instruct the model to state 'I don't know' if the answer cannot be found in the provided context?
exercises.explain: Forcing the model to say 'I don't know' prevents it from guessing or hallucinating when the context doesn't contain the answer.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe what we want the LLM to ignore when answering grounded questions.
exercises.sentence: To keep answers accurate, we force the LLM to rely on the provided context instead of its parametric memory.
exercises.explain: An LLM's parametric memory contains the general knowledge it learned during training, which we must bypass to ensure grounded answers.

exercises.prompt:
Context: 'Thank you for shopping with us. Please note that all sales are final.'
Question: 'How do I return an item?'
Which of these responses shows a failure of grounding (relying on parametric memory instead)?

options.text: Based on the context, we do not accept returns. All sales are final.
I cannot answer this because the context states all sales are final, and no returns are mentioned.
You can return your item within 30 days for a full refund, as long as you have the original receipt.

exercises.explain: Option C demonstrates a classic hallucination where the model uses external knowledge (a typical refund policy) instead of adhering strictly to the 'all sales are final' rule in the provided context.

exercises.prompt: You want to build a customer support bot that never lies about your company's return policy. How should you structure your prompt instructions to achieve this?

options.text: Tell the model to write a creative story using its general knowledge first.
Instruct the model to answer only using the provided facts and output a specific fallback phrase if the answer is missing.
Remove the system prompt entirely and let the model decide how to answer.
Ask the model to search the live web instead of reading the provided context.

exercises.explain: To enforce strict grounding, you must explicitly command the model to rely only on the provided context, avoid using outside information, and truthfully admit when the answer cannot be found in the text. This prevents the model from falling back on its parametric memory.
